unitTitle: Y7 Networks — from semaphores to the Internet (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Computer networks and protocols
lessons.objectives:
I can say what a computer network is
I can explain that data is passed from device to device
I can say what a protocol is
I can give an example of a non-networking protocol
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: network · protocol · relay · stand-alone · HTTP. TA: each pupil has the starter and activity worksheets. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — sending messages long ago. Semaphore-tower image on the board; pupils TICK the ways that do NOT use a computer (multi-select, no writing).
   • Support: choose how a semaphore worked.  • Core: write one old way to send a message.  • Challenge: link semaphore relay to a message crossing the internet.
2. (8–18) What is a network? I-do → we-do. A network = two or more computers connected. Stand-alone = on its own. Pupils name things they do that use a network.
3. (18–30) A message to Australia. A message is PASSED ON device to device (like a letter via depots), not sent straight there. Likely error: thinks email “goes straight” → fix-words “it hops through many devices.”
4. (30–42) What is a protocol? A set of rules both sides follow. You-do: match the climber/belayer commands to their meanings on the activity worksheet (a non-networking protocol).
   • Support: match command ↔ meaning.  • Core: explain what protocol means.  • Challenge: give an email-address rule. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (42–50) Computer protocols (email needs @, web address starts http://). Pupils screenshot/photograph their ordered climber commands.
6. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; share one rule a protocol gives. Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Networking hardware
lessons.objectives:
I can name the main pieces of network hardware
I can say what each piece of hardware does
I can choose hardware to build a simple network
I can say what an ISP is
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: network cable · hub · server · router · ISP · ports. TA: starter and activity worksheets ready; the “build a network” task on screen if available. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what am I? Match each hardware NAME to what it does (matching widget; hub image on the board). No writing.
   • Support: choose that a hub has ports.  • Core: say what a server does.  • Challenge: why a hub means fewer cables.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do. Cable carries messages (copper wires). Hub connects computers in one building, has ports, sends a message to ALL. Fill-in-the-blanks on the activity worksheet.
3. (18–32) The server (image). A powerful computer providing a service; a file server stores shared files centrally. Link to the school shared drive. Likely error: thinks files live on each PC → fix-words “the files are on the server.”
4. (32–45) You-do — build the network: add a hub, then a file server, then a router so four PCs share files and reach the internet.
   • Support: a printed block-order strip.  • Core: build it.  • Challenge: count cables saved. TA: “prompt, do not build it for them.” Pupils screenshot their network.
5. (45–52) The router and the ISP. A router joins networks (home → internet); the connection comes from an ISP.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; name one piece of hardware and its job. Same task for all; low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Wired and wireless networks
lessons.objectives:
I can compare wired and wireless connections
I can name wireless technologies
I can say what bandwidth means and its units
I can choose wired or wireless for a situation
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: wired · wireless · WiFi · bandwidth · bits per second · buffering. TA: starter and activity worksheets ready; speedtest.net open if used. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — wired or wireless? Pupils TICK the home devices that are usually wireless (multi-select). No writing.
   • Support: choose that wireless uses radio waves.  • Core: name a wireless technology.  • Challenge: why wire a games console.
2. (8–18) I-do → we-do. Wired = data along cables; wireless = radio waves. Names: Bluetooth, WiFi, 3G/4G/5G.
3. (18–30) Bandwidth = data moved in a given time, measured in bits per second. Likely error: “more bandwidth = faster” → fix-words “a WIDER pipe, more water at once, not faster water.”
4. (30–40) Test the speed (image). Optional: pupils run speedtest.net and read upload/download; screenshot the result. Skip if short on time — it does not affect the objectives.
5. (40–50) You-do — wired or wireless? Tick the high-bandwidth activities and the wireless situations on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: decide on portability.  • Core: define bandwidth.  • Challenge: a disadvantage of wired. TA: “prompt, do not choose for them.”
6. (50–55) Plenary/exit ticket — self-tick the four “I can…”; define bandwidth. Same task for all; low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound.

lessons.title: The internet
lessons.objectives:
I can say what the internet is
I can explain how data crosses the internet in packets
I can describe a packet (header, payload, IP address)
I can name the protocols TCP and IP
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: internet · packet · IP address · header · payload · TCP · IP. TA: starter and activity worksheets ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is the internet? Single-choice definition + multi-select “tick the internet activities”. No writing.
   • Support: choose where most data travels.  • Core: one sentence on what the internet is.  • Challenge: why “a network of networks”.
2. (8–18) The internet is global (image). A network of networks; 99% of cross-continent data goes through sea cables. Optional teacher-played hook (motion/sound — teacher’s choice): the Vint Cerf video.
3. (18–30) Data travels in packets (parcels image). A message is split into packets, each taking its own route. Optional hook: “A Packet’s Tale” video. Header (addresses + sequence number) vs payload (a piece of the message).
4. (30–42) IP addresses — four numbers 0–255 with dots, unique to a device. You-do: fill in the missing packet parts and read the jumbled message. Likely error: forgets the sequence number → fix-words “how do we put them back in order?” TA: “prompt, do not fill it for them.” Screenshot the packets.
5. (42–52) Protocols — TCP (splits + reorders) and IP (routes using addresses). Plenary multiple-choice questions.
6. (52–55) Self-tick the four “I can…”. Same task for all; low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Internet services
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the difference between the internet and the World Wide Web
I can name internet services
I can say what the Internet of Things is
I can describe a privacy or security risk of IoT
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: internet · World Wide Web · service · email · VoIP · IoT · privacy · security. TA: starter and activity worksheets ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–6) Starter — internet or World Wide Web? Single-choice. No writing.
   • Support: the internet is the hardware.  • Core: one difference.  • Challenge: explain why they are not the same.
2. (6–18) The internet (hardware) vs the WWW (web pages on it) — connected-world image. The web is ONE service. Internet services: web, email, IM, VoIP, streaming, IoT.
3. (18–28) VoIP (voice over the internet) and email (lots is spam). Misconception: web = internet → fix-words “the web is a service ON the internet.”
4. (28–40) The Internet of Things — everyday objects connected to the internet. Optional teacher-played hook (motion/sound — teacher’s choice): the IoT video. IoT is helpful but risks PRIVACY and SECURITY.
5. (40–50) You-do — match the service; tick the real IoT risks on the activity worksheet. Screenshot the matched services.
   • Support: match the two you know.  • Core: name two services + define IoT.  • Challenge: a benefit AND a risk. TA: “prompt, do not match it for them.”
6. (50–55) Exit ticket — self-tick the four “I can…”; difference between internet and web. Same task for all; calm, factual tone. No flashing/sound.

lessons.title: The World Wide Web
lessons.objectives:
I can name the parts of the World Wide Web
I can explain the difference between HTTP and HTTPS
I can describe what a URL is
I can say what the domain name system does
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: web browser · web server · web page · search engine · HTTP · HTTPS · URL · domain name · DNS. TA: starter and activity worksheets ready; the end-of-unit assessment follows. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — browser, search engine, or website? Browser-logos image; single-choice. No writing.
   • Support: a browser is software.  • Core: the job of a browser.  • Challenge: what a search engine does.
2. (8–20) Browser (reads HTML, shows the page) and server (stores websites, sends pages). HTTP (plain text) vs HTTPS (encrypted, secure) — padlock image. Likely error: “HTTP is fine for passwords” → fix-words “passwords need HTTPS.”
3. (20–32) URLs — http (protocol) · www (the web) · facebook.com (domain name). Top-level domains (.com, .gov, .co.uk).
4. (32–42) Domain names and DNS — DNS maps an easy domain name to a device’s IP address (link back to lesson 4). You-do: match the terms; decide HTTP or HTTPS on the activity worksheet; screenshot a real address bar.
   • Support: match the two you know.  • Core: HTTP vs HTTPS; what a URL is.  • Challenge: why a payment needs HTTPS. TA: “prompt, do not match it for them.”
5. (42–52) End-of-unit assessment (multiple-choice across all six lessons); early finishers do the explorer activity.
6. (52–55) Self-tick the four “I can…”. Same task for all; low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound.
